questions.stem: A parametrized differentiable curve in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is, by definition:
options: a continuous map whose image is a closed set.
a map $\alpha : \mathbb{R}^3 \to I$ onto an interval.
any subset of $\mathbb{R}^3$ that looks like a line locally.
a differentiable map $\alpha : I \to \mathbb{R}^3$ from an open interval $I \subset \mathbb{R}$.
questions.explanation: In do Carmo's setup a parametrized differentiable curve is a differentiable (infinitely differentiable) map $\alpha : I \to \mathbb{R}^3$ where $I = (a,b)$ is an open interval. The curve is the map, not just its image (the trace), and the domain is the interval.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The trace of a parametrized curve $\alpha : I \to \mathbb{R}^3$ is:
questions.explanation: The trace is the image $\alpha(I)$, the set of points in $\mathbb{R}^3$ swept out by the curve. Different parametrizations can share the same trace, so the curve (the map) carries more information than its trace.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A parametrized curve $\alpha : I \to \mathbb{R}^3$ is called regular at $t$ when:
questions.explanation: Regularity means the velocity vector does not vanish: $\alpha'(t) \ne 0$ for all $t \in I$. This guarantees a well-defined tangent line at every point. The condition is on the derivative, not on the position vector.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the straight line $\alpha(t) = p + t v$ with $p, v \in \mathbb{R}^3$, the curve is regular if and only if:
questions.explanation: Here $\alpha'(t) = v$ for all $t$, so regularity ($\alpha' \ne 0$) holds exactly when $v \ne 0$. The base point $p$ and any orthogonality have no bearing on regularity.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Consider $\alpha(t) = (t^2, t^3)$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$ (a semicubical parabola). At which parameter value does it fail to be regular?
questions.explanation: We have $\alpha'(t) = (2t, 3t^2)$, so $\alpha'(0) = (0,0)$. The velocity vanishes only at $t = 0$, where the trace has a cusp. Everywhere else $\alpha'(t) \ne 0$, so the curve is regular except at $t = 0$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The arc length of a regular curve $\alpha$ measured from a point $t_0$ is given by:
questions.explanation: Arc length is the integral of speed: $s(t) = \int_{t_0}^{t} |\alpha'(u)|\, du$. The straight-line distance $|\alpha(t) - \alpha(t_0)|$ undercounts whenever the curve bends, and the second derivative is unrelated to length.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the circle $\alpha(t) = (a\cos t, a\sin t)$ with $a > 0$, the speed $|\alpha'(t)|$ equals:
questions.explanation: Differentiating gives $\alpha'(t) = (-a\sin t, a\cos t)$, so $|\alpha'(t)| = \sqrt{a^2\sin^2 t + a^2\cos^2 t} = \sqrt{a^2} = a$. The speed is the constant $a$; the value $2\pi a$ is the full circumference, not the speed.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The length of the circle $\alpha(t) = (3\cos t, 3\sin t)$, $t \in [0, 2\pi]$, is:
questions.explanation: The speed is $|\alpha'| = 3$, so the length is $\int_0^{2\pi} 3\, dt = 3 \cdot 2\pi = 6\pi$. Equivalently, the circumference of a circle of radius $3$ is $2\pi(3) = 6\pi$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the circular helix $\alpha(t) = (a\cos t, a\sin t, b t)$, the speed $|\alpha'(t)|$ is:
questions.explanation: Here $\alpha'(t) = (-a\sin t, a\cos t, b)$, so $|\alpha'(t)| = \sqrt{a^2\sin^2 t + a^2\cos^2 t + b^2} = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}$. The speed is constant, independent of $t$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Over one full turn $t \in [0, 2\pi]$, the helix $\alpha(t) = (3\cos t, 3\sin t, 4 t)$ has length:
questions.explanation: The speed is $\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5$, so the length is $\int_0^{2\pi} 5\, dt = 5 \cdot 2\pi = 10\pi$. The $3$-$4$-$5$ triangle makes the speed exactly $5$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A regular curve is said to be parametrized by arc length (unit speed) when:
questions.explanation: Arc-length parametrization means the speed is identically $1$: $|\alpha'(s)| = 1$. Then the parameter $s$ measures length directly, since $\int |\alpha'|\,du = \int 1\,du$ equals the change in $s$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Why can every regular curve be reparametrized by arc length?
options: Because $|\alpha'(t)| > 0$, so $s(t)$ is strictly increasing and hence invertible.
Because its trace is always a straight line.
questions.explanation: Regularity gives $\tfrac{ds}{dt} = |\alpha'(t)| > 0$, so the arc-length function $s(t)$ is strictly increasing and therefore has a differentiable inverse $t(s)$. Setting $\beta(s) = \alpha(t(s))$ yields a unit-speed reparametrization.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Reparametrizing the helix $\alpha(t) = (3\cos t, 3\sin t, 4 t)$ by arc length $s$ gives $\beta(s) = \alpha(t(s))$. What is $t(s)$?
questions.explanation: Since the speed is $5$, arc length is $s = 5t$, so $t = s/5$. Then $\beta(s) = (3\cos(s/5), 3\sin(s/5), 4s/5)$, and one checks $|\beta'(s)| = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: If $\alpha$ is parametrized by arc length, then the unit tangent vector $T(s)$ equals:
questions.explanation: For a unit-speed curve $|\alpha'(s)| = 1$, so the unit tangent is simply $T(s) = \alpha'(s)$ (no normalization needed). For an arbitrary parametrization one would divide by the speed.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For a general regular curve $\alpha(t)$ (not necessarily unit speed), the unit tangent vector is:
questions.explanation: The unit tangent always points in the direction of motion and has length $1$, so $T(t) = \alpha'(t)/|\alpha'(t)|$. Only when the curve is already unit speed does this reduce to $\alpha'(t)$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For the helix $\alpha(t) = (a\cos t, a\sin t, b t)$, the unit tangent vector $T(t)$ is:
questions.explanation: We have $\alpha'(t) = (-a\sin t, a\cos t, b)$ with $|\alpha'(t)| = \sqrt{a^2+b^2}$, so $T(t) = \alpha'/|\alpha'| = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}(-a\sin t, a\cos t, b)$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Compute the arc length of the logarithmic spiral $\alpha(t) = (e^t\cos t, e^t\sin t)$ from $t = 0$ to $t = \ln 2$.
questions.explanation: Differentiating, $\alpha'(t) = (e^t(\cos t - \sin t), e^t(\sin t + \cos t))$, so $|\alpha'(t)|^2 = e^{2t}[(\cos t - \sin t)^2 + (\sin t + \cos t)^2] = e^{2t}\cdot 2$. Thus $|\alpha'| = \sqrt{2}\, e^t$ and the length is $\int_0^{\ln 2} \sqrt{2}\, e^t\, dt = \sqrt{2}(e^{\ln 2} - 1) = \sqrt{2}(2 - 1) = \sqrt{2}$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Two parametrized curves are reparametrizations of one another when they differ by:
options: scaling the trace by a positive constant.
an orthogonal change of coordinates in $\mathbb{R}^3$.
a differentiable change of parameter $t = h(u)$ with $h' \ne 0$.
adding a constant vector to the position.
questions.explanation: A reparametrization replaces the parameter by a differentiable bijection $t = h(u)$ with nowhere-zero derivative ($h' \ne 0$), so the new curve $\beta(u) = \alpha(h(u))$ has the same trace and stays regular. Rigid motions and scalings change the curve in space, not merely its parametrization.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The tangent line to a regular curve $\alpha$ at the point $\alpha(t_0)$ is the line through $\alpha(t_0)$ in the direction of:
questions.explanation: The tangent line passes through $\alpha(t_0)$ with direction the velocity $\alpha'(t_0)$, which is nonzero by regularity. Its parametrization is $r(u) = \alpha(t_0) + u\,\alpha'(t_0)$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: The curve $\alpha(t) = (t, t^2)$ (a parabola) is regular on all of $\mathbb{R}$ because:
options: $\alpha'(t) = (1, 2t)$ has first component $1$, so it is never zero.
questions.explanation: Regularity needs $\alpha'(t) \ne 0$. Since $\alpha'(t) = (1, 2t)$ always has first coordinate $1$, the velocity can never be the zero vector, so the parabola is regular everywhere. Boundedness of the trace and the value of $\alpha''$ are irrelevant to regularity.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Consider the circular helix $\alpha(t) = (2\cos t, 2\sin t, t)$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
questions.solution: Differentiating gives $\alpha'(t) = (-2\sin t, 2\cos t, 1)$. The speed $|\alpha'(t)| = \sqrt{4 + 1}$ equals {{1}} (rounded to two decimals). Because this speed is constant and positive, the curve {{2}}. The arc length from $t = 0$ to $t = 4$ equals {{3}} (two decimals).
questions.explanation: $\alpha'(t) = (-2\sin t, 2\cos t, 1)$ has $|\alpha'| = \sqrt{4\sin^2 t + 4\cos^2 t + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$. The speed is never zero, so the helix is regular. Arc length over $[0,4]$ is $\int_0^4 \sqrt{5}\, dt = 4\sqrt{5} \approx 8.94$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Analyse the plane curve $\alpha(t) = (t^3, t^2)$ for $t \in \mathbb{R}$.
questions.solution: The velocity is $\alpha'(t) = (3t^2, 2t)$. Setting both components to zero, $\alpha'(t) = 0$ only at the parameter value $t =$ {{1}}. Therefore the curve fails to be regular at exactly one point, and the trace has a {{2}} there. Away from that value the curve is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\alpha'(t) = (3t^2, 2t) = (0,0)$ forces $t = 0$. At $t = 0$ the velocity vanishes and the trace shows a cusp. For $t \ne 0$ the velocity is nonzero, so the curve is regular there.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: A particle moves along $\alpha(t) = (a\cos t, a\sin t)$, a circle of radius $a > 0$, for $t \in [0, 2\pi]$.
questions.solution: The speed is $|\alpha'(t)| =$ {{1}} (in terms of $a$). Hence the total length traced over one full period is the circumference {{2}}. Since the speed is constant, the parameter $t$ {{3}} proportional to arc length.
questions.explanation: $\alpha'(t) = (-a\sin t, a\cos t)$ gives $|\alpha'| = a$. The length over $[0,2\pi]$ is $\int_0^{2\pi} a\, dt = 2\pi a$, the circumference. A constant speed means $s(t) = a t$ is proportional to $t$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Reparametrize the helix $\alpha(t) = (3\cos t, 3\sin t, 4 t)$ by arc length measured from $t = 0$.
questions.solution: The constant speed is $|\alpha'(t)| =$ {{1}}. Thus arc length is $s = 5t$, so $t = s/5$ and the unit-speed curve is $\beta(s) = \alpha(s/5)$. Differentiating, $|\beta'(s)|$ equals {{2}}, confirming unit speed.
questions.explanation: $\alpha'(t) = (-3\sin t, 3\cos t, 4)$ gives $|\alpha'| = \sqrt{9 + 16} = 5$. So $s = 5t$, $t = s/5$, and $\beta(s) = (3\cos(s/5), 3\sin(s/5), 4s/5)$ has $\beta'(s) = (-\tfrac35\sin(s/5), \tfrac35\cos(s/5), \tfrac45)$ with $|\beta'| = \sqrt{\tfrac{9}{25} + \tfrac{16}{25}} = 1$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: Find the arc length of the astroid arc $\alpha(t) = (\cos^3 t, \sin^3 t)$ over the first quadrant $t \in [0, \pi/2]$.
questions.solution: Here $\alpha'(t) = (-3\cos^2 t\,\sin t,\; 3\sin^2 t\,\cos t)$, so $|\alpha'(t)| = 3|\sin t\cos t| = \tfrac{3}{2}|\sin 2t|$, which on $[0,\pi/2]$ {{1}} negative. Integrating, the arc length equals {{2}} (a number).
questions.explanation: $|\alpha'| = 3\sqrt{\cos^4 t\sin^2 t + \sin^4 t\cos^2 t} = 3|\sin t\cos t| = \tfrac32|\sin 2t|$. On $[0,\pi/2]$ we have $2t \in [0,\pi]$ where $\sin 2t \ge 0$, so it is never negative. Then length $= \int_0^{\pi/2}\tfrac32\sin 2t\, dt = \tfrac32\big[-\tfrac{\cos 2t}{2}\big]_0^{\pi/2} = \tfrac32\cdot 1 = \tfrac32$.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces

questions.stem: For a general regular curve $\alpha(t)$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$, recall how the unit tangent is built.
questions.solution: The velocity vector is $\alpha'(t)$, whose length is the {{1}}. To obtain the unit tangent $T(t)$ one divides the velocity by its length, giving $T(t) = \alpha'(t)/|\alpha'(t)|$; by construction $|T(t)|$ equals {{2}}. This division is legitimate precisely because regularity guarantees $|\alpha'(t)|$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The length $|\alpha'(t)|$ is the speed. Dividing the velocity by its own length yields a vector of length $1$, so $|T(t)| = 1$. The division requires $|\alpha'(t)| \ne 0$, which is exactly the regularity condition.
questions.source: in the style of do Carmo, Differential Geometry of Curves and Surfaces
